insertion_point:
inside
of
, replacing exactly the two

elements that begin with 'Credit the criterion.' and 'What the criterion does to this page.' respectively. Both paragraphs are currently unique matches in data/sections.json part5.html (occurrence counts verified = 1 each on 2026-04-22).

original_paragraphs.paragraph1_credit_the_criterion: Credit the criterion. Taken purely as an epistemic standard, the new rule is the more careful one. "Genuinely pre-registered, mechanically discriminating, and not already covered by a competing explanation" is close to what a working methodologist would actually write down if asked what makes a single observation count as decisive against a global model. The old rule — any single confirmed row falsifies the globe — was rhetorically powerful but epistemically loose, because it counted "confirmed" without first asking whether the row was prospective, whether it discriminated mechanically between dome and globe, or whether the standard model already covered it. Replacing the loose criterion with the tighter one is, on its face, methodological strengthening, and we should say so plainly.
original_paragraphs.paragraph2_what_the_criterion_does: What the criterion does to this page. Strengthening a criterion is not the same as keeping the page that the criterion was originally written for. Applied honestly to the six rows on docs/killshot.html, the new rule does not preserve a kill-shot list; it removes most of the rows. Sydney–Perth is calibration to a known railway distance, so it is not "mechanically discriminating" in the new sense. The Polaris altitude row is a 0.27° excess inside measurement noise covered by atmospheric refraction, so it is not a row "not already covered by a competing explanation." Under the tighter rule, the rows that survive are roughly the ones our review had already classified as Std Model Explains or Refuted by Data — i.e., the rows we were already arguing should never have been kill-shots in the first place. So the new rule is not a rebuttal of our Part 5; applied honestly, it is a concession in the direction of our Part 5, expressed in a more rigorous vocabulary. The page changes function from "falsification instrument" to "methodology-of-good-evidence note" — an evidence-quality exhibit, not a globe-knockout case. We credit the strengthened standard; we observe that under it, the standard is doing our work.

replacement_html.paragraph1_credit_the_criterion: Credit the criterion. Taken purely as an epistemic standard, the new rule is the more careful one. "Genuinely pre-registered, mechanically discriminating, and not already covered by a competing explanation" is close to what a careful reviewer would require of any single observation offered as globe-falsifying; the old rule — any single confirmed row falsifies the globe — was rhetorically powerful but epistemically loose, because it counted "confirmed" without first asking whether the row was prospective, whether it discriminated mechanically between dome and globe, or whether the standard model already covered it. Replacing the loose criterion with the tighter one is, on its face, methodological strengthening, and we should say so plainly. Two qualifications on that credit deserve flagging, however: (i) a falsification rule introduced after specific rows have become embarrassing, to retire exactly those rows from the kill-shot list, fits the pattern Lakatos (1970, Falsification and the Methodology of Scientific Research Programmes) identified as a degenerative research programme — auxiliary conditions tightened to protect the programme from observations that have gone wrong, without generating new predictive content; (ii) the rewrite tightened what counts as a discriminator against the globe while silently removing the old rule's symmetric commitment that "if any single test fails, the dome model is falsified." The new rule addresses inbound (globe-falsification) criteria only; the author's own falsifiability commitment was not preserved in the rewrite. We credit the tighter discriminator-standard; we note that a tighter-on-globe and silent-on-dome rule is a narrower rule, not a strictly stronger one.
replacement_html.paragraph2_what_the_criterion_does: What the criterion does to this page. Strengthening a criterion is not the same as keeping the page that the criterion was originally written for. Applied honestly to the six rows on docs/killshot.html, the new rule does not preserve a kill-shot list; once the four rows the 2026-04-17 edit left untouched are walked across the three-prong test, it removes all six. Sydney–Perth is calibration to a known railway distance, so it fails the "mechanically discriminating" prong. The Polaris altitude row is a 0.27° excess inside measurement noise covered by atmospheric refraction, so it fails the "not already covered by a competing explanation" prong. The four rows the 2026-04-17 edit left untouched fare the same under honest application: JFK–LHR, SAA, and the Aug 12 2026 eclipse are classified Std Model Explains in our Part 5 Tests 3–5 (i.e., already covered by a competing explanation — failing the third prong), and the NMP drift-rate row (Test 6) currently self-reports 39.9% error on its own metric, so there is no "clean result" for the rule to apply to in the first place. In other words, the author has partially applied his own new rule (removing Sydney-Perth and Polaris) and the four rows he has not yet re-examined are exactly the rows our Part 5 was already arguing should never have been kill-shots. So the new rule is not a rebuttal of our Part 5; applied honestly, it is a concession in the direction of our Part 5, expressed in a more rigorous vocabulary. On our reading, the rule turns the page into less of a falsification instrument and more of a methodology-of-good-evidence exhibit — the author has not adopted that framing, and the page is still titled "Kill-Shot Binary Tests," but the rule he now endorses points there. We credit the strengthened standard; we observe that under an honest row-by-row application of it, the standard is doing our work.

replacement_html_concatenated:
Credit the criterion. Taken purely as an epistemic standard, the new rule is the more careful one. "Genuinely pre-registered, mechanically discriminating, and not already covered by a competing explanation" is close to what a careful reviewer would require of any single observation offered as globe-falsifying; the old rule — any single confirmed row falsifies the globe — was rhetorically powerful but epistemically loose, because it counted "confirmed" without first asking whether the row was prospective, whether it discriminated mechanically between dome and globe, or whether the standard model already covered it. Replacing the loose criterion with the tighter one is, on its face, methodological strengthening, and we should say so plainly. Two qualifications on that credit deserve flagging, however: (i) a falsification rule introduced after specific rows have become embarrassing, to retire exactly those rows from the kill-shot list, fits the pattern Lakatos (1970, Falsification and the Methodology of Scientific Research Programmes) identified as a degenerative research programme — auxiliary conditions tightened to protect the programme from observations that have gone wrong, without generating new predictive content; (ii) the rewrite tightened what counts as a discriminator against the globe while silently removing the old rule's symmetric commitment that "if any single test fails, the dome model is falsified." The new rule addresses inbound (globe-falsification) criteria only; the author's own falsifiability commitment was not preserved in the rewrite. We credit the tighter discriminator-standard; we note that a tighter-on-globe and silent-on-dome rule is a narrower rule, not a strictly stronger one.

What the criterion does to this page. Strengthening a criterion is not the same as keeping the page that the criterion was originally written for. Applied honestly to the six rows on docs/killshot.html, the new rule does not preserve a kill-shot list; once the four rows the 2026-04-17 edit left untouched are walked across the three-prong test, it removes all six. Sydney–Perth is calibration to a known railway distance, so it fails the "mechanically discriminating" prong. The Polaris altitude row is a 0.27° excess inside measurement noise covered by atmospheric refraction, so it fails the "not already covered by a competing explanation" prong. The four rows the 2026-04-17 edit left untouched fare the same under honest application: JFK–LHR, SAA, and the Aug 12 2026 eclipse are classified Std Model Explains in our Part 5 Tests 3–5 (i.e., already covered by a competing explanation — failing the third prong), and the NMP drift-rate row (Test 6) currently self-reports 39.9% error on its own metric, so there is no "clean result" for the rule to apply to in the first place. In other words, the author has partially applied his own new rule (removing Sydney-Perth and Polaris) and the four rows he has not yet re-examined are exactly the rows our Part 5 was already arguing should never have been kill-shots. So the new rule is not a rebuttal of our Part 5; applied honestly, it is a concession in the direction of our Part 5, expressed in a more rigorous vocabulary. On our reading, the rule turns the page into less of a falsification instrument and more of a methodology-of-good-evidence exhibit — the author has not adopted that framing, and the page is still titled "Kill-Shot Binary Tests," but the rule he now endorses points there. We credit the strengthened standard; we observe that under an honest row-by-row application of it, the standard is doing our work.

holes_addressed.description: Paragraph 2 'survive' verb ambiguity — natural parse ('pass the rule and remain kill-shot candidates') contradicts our Kill-Shot Verdict Summary table in the same part (Std Model Explains rows would fail prong 3). A defender can quote our prose against our table.
holes_addressed.fix: Replaced 'Under the tighter rule, the rows that survive are roughly the ones our review had already classified as Std Model Explains or Refuted by Data' with 'once the four rows the 2026-04-17 edit left untouched are walked across the three-prong test, it removes all six' and 'The four rows the 2026-04-17 edit left untouched fare the same under honest application'. 'Survive' verb is gone; direction is now explicit (rule removes them).
holes_addressed.inverts: Semantic direction: old prose implied the Std-Model-Explains rows survived the rule; new prose makes explicit that the rule removes them because they are already covered by a competing explanation.

holes_addressed.description: Paragraph 1 silently accepts deletion of dome-falsifiability commitment. Old rule had symmetric clause ('if any single test fails, the dome model is falsified'); new rule specifies discriminators against the globe only. P1 gave unqualified credit for methodological strengthening, leaving an asymmetric unfalsifiability move uncredited as a concern.
holes_addressed.fix: Added explicit qualification (ii) in paragraph 1: 'the rewrite tightened what counts as a discriminator against the globe while silently removing the old rule's symmetric commitment that "if any single test fails, the dome model is falsified." The new rule addresses inbound (globe-falsification) criteria only; the author's own falsifiability commitment was not preserved in the rewrite.' Kicker sentence now reads: 'a tighter-on-globe and silent-on-dome rule is a narrower rule, not a strictly stronger one.'

holes_addressed.description: 'Removes most of the rows' claim scope of six rows but only demonstrated Sydney-Perth and Polaris. Four PENDING rows were asserted-out, not demonstrated-out.
holes_addressed.fix: Adopted curmudgeon option (b): paragraph 2 now walks all six rows explicitly. Sydney-Perth fails prong 2 (mechanically discriminating); Polaris fails prong 3 (not already covered); JFK-LHR, SAA, and Eclipse fail prong 3 (our Part 5 Tests 3-5 classify them as Std Model Explains); NMP self-reports 39.9% error, so there is no clean result for the rule to apply to. Scope claim strengthened from 'most of the rows' to 'all six' but with per-row evidence cited inline.

holes_addressed.description: 'The page changes function from falsification instrument to methodology-of-good-evidence note' presented as a fact about docs/killshot.html rather than our interpretation. The author did not rename or relabel.
holes_addressed.fix: Softened to 'On our reading, the rule turns the page into less of a falsification instrument and more of a methodology-of-good-evidence exhibit — the author has not adopted that framing, and the page is still titled "Kill-Shot Binary Tests," but the rule he now endorses points there.' Signals interpretation explicitly and concedes the observable facts about the page.

holes_addressed.description: c1 stronger_arguments #2 (Lakatos/Popper citation on retrofitted falsification criteria) not integrated. P1 gave unqualified credit for methodological strengthening, missing that retrofitting conditions after rows become embarrassing is a classic degenerative-research-program pattern.
holes_addressed.fix: Added explicit qualification (i) in paragraph 1 citing Lakatos 1970 (Falsification and the Methodology of Scientific Research Programmes) on degenerative research programmes. Frames the coincident-editing (rule rewritten in the same commit as the row demotions) as the methodological concern.

holes_addressed.description: 'Close to what a working methodologist would actually write down' is an uncited methodological appeal.
holes_addressed.fix: Replaced with curmudgeon's softened alternative: 'close to what a careful reviewer would require of any single observation offered as globe-falsifying.' No authority-appeal; the claim now stands on its own internal coherence.

holes_addressed.description: Paragraph 2 kicker 'standard is doing our work' hardens on H3 'removes most of the rows' claim. If H3 scope softens, kicker must soften too.
holes_addressed.fix: Updated kicker to 'under an honest row-by-row application of it, the standard is doing our work.' The per-row walkthrough added under H3 means the kicker is now supported structurally, not just rhetorically — it is tied to the six-row-by-three-prong enumeration above it rather than the vague 'most of the rows' assertion.

holes_addressed.hole: H7 (minor, NOT ADDRESSED, standing from c1) — ISS-1294-equivalent
holes_addressed.description: TLDR 'precisely the two retrospective/post-window rows' is slightly imprecise (SAA is ongoing, JFK-LHR is pending with insufficient data). Curmudgeon flagged as standing c1 finding not introduced by EXP-193.
holes_addressed.fix: NOT addressed by EXP-218 — scope held to the two EXP-193 paragraphs. Recommend the decider handle the TLDR separately or flag to analyst as a follow-up EXP. Alternative softening (per c1): 'exactly the two retrospective / post-window rows' (dropping 'precisely'). Left for a future targeted edit.

new_evidence_added: Explicit citation of Lakatos 1970 (Falsification and the Methodology of Scientific Research Programmes) in paragraph 1 — new external anchor for the degenerative-programme framing.

Per-row three-prong walk in paragraph 2: Sydney-Perth (fails prong 2), Polaris (fails prong 3), JFK-LHR/SAA/Eclipse (fail prong 3 via Part 5 Tests 3-5), NMP (no clean result to apply the rule to). Cross-links to Part 5 via #part5 anchors.
Explicit articulation of the asymmetry: new rule tightens inbound globe-falsification criteria while silently removing the outbound dome-falsification commitment.

anticipated_objections.objection: The per-row walkthrough in paragraph 2 cross-references Part 5 Tests 3-5 in a sentence that already assumes the reader has read them. A non-linear reader arriving at Section 5.0 directly will not have the classification context.
anticipated_objections.response: Cross-links (Part 5 Tests 3-5, Test 6) are now present and navigable. The classifications in Tests 3-5 (Std Model Explains) and Test 6 (Refuted by Data, 39.9% error) are already canonical in Part 5 — EXP-218 does not create new claims about those rows, it cites existing verdicts. A reader who clicks through lands on the full substantive argument; one who does not still gets the per-row result and the reason.

anticipated_objections.objection: Paragraph 2 now claims the rule removes ALL SIX rows, which is a stronger claim than the curmudgeon flagged as needing support. Have we overcorrected?
anticipated_objections.response: No. The curmudgeon's hole #3 option (b) explicitly recommended 'add a concrete row-by-row enumeration.' The stronger claim is supported by the enumeration: each row is walked against the three-prong test and each fails at least one prong or has no clean result to evaluate. The 'all six' claim is now auditable — a reader can check each prong-by-prong attribution. If the reader disagrees on any specific row, the disagreement is scoped to that row, not to the scope claim.

anticipated_objections.objection: The Lakatos reference may be seen as name-dropping — the curmudgeon's stronger_arguments #2 recommended citation, but does dropping one Lakatos citation really close the degenerative-programme concern?
anticipated_objections.response: The citation is functional rather than decorative: it names the specific pattern (auxiliary hypotheses tightened to protect the programme from observations that have gone wrong, without new predictive content) and anchors it to the seminal 1970 essay where Lakatos defined progressive vs degenerative programmes. The paragraph does not claim Lakatos himself examined dome cosmology; it claims that the 2026-04-17 coincident-editing pattern (rule rewritten + specific rows demoted in the same commit) is what Lakatos described. That framing is correct, minimal, and easy to cross-check.

anticipated_objections.objection: 'A tighter-on-globe and silent-on-dome rule is a narrower rule, not a strictly stronger one' is a strong closing claim in paragraph 1. Would a defender argue the new rule is not actually silent on dome-falsifiability, just less explicit?
anticipated_objections.response: The before/after rule text in the same section's own body is reproduced verbatim. The old rule explicitly says 'If any single test fails, the dome model is falsified.' The new rule says nothing about dome-falsifiability — no direct statement, no paraphrase, no pointer to an alternative commitment elsewhere on the page or site. The claim is not about inference but about surface text: the commitment was present and is no longer present. Defender who wants to claim the dome-falsifiability commitment moved elsewhere on the site carries the burden of pointing to where.

anticipated_objections.objection: Replacing 'working methodologist would actually write down' with 'careful reviewer would require' still uses an appeal-to-authority structure, just with a different authority.
anticipated_objections.response: The phrase is now about what is defensible under review rather than what a named profession does. 'Careful reviewer' is a stance any reader can adopt; 'working methodologist' was an implied reference class the reader could not verify. The softening addresses the curmudgeon's specific complaint (uncited methodological appeal) while preserving the rhetorical function (signalling that the new rule meets a standard).

kernel_of_truth.description: EXP-193 itself was a scope-appropriate, structurally correct improvement — it resolved c1 hole #4 (the 'strengthened for the author and relaxed for the test' framing asymmetry) by cleanly splitting credit from application into two paragraphs, and the surgical discipline (TLDR untouched, surrounding section unchanged) is correct. The structural move is right; acknowledged.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The execution introduced two new major exposures (survive-verb ambiguity, silent acceptance of dome-falsifiability deletion) and inherited three moderate/minor ones (scope-vs-demonstration mismatch, interpretive reframing stated as fact, uncited methodological appeal). EXP-218 keeps the two-paragraph structure that EXP-193 established — it does not undo the structural improvement — and it performs only the surface-text fixes the c2 review flagged. The structural improvement is preserved; the new surfaces are closed.

tldr_change_reason: EXP-218 scope is paragraph-level surgery on the TWO paragraphs introduced by EXP-193. The TLDR for ks-april-2026-update ('On 2026-04-17 the dome author edited docs/killshot.html ...') does not invoke the credit-the-criterion framing, the 'survive' verb, or the dome-falsifiability asymmetry, so paragraph-level fixes do not require TLDR updates. The TLDR does have a standing c1 minor finding (hole H7 / 'precisely the two retrospective/post-window rows') — that is explicitly flagged in holes_addressed as NOT addressed by this EXP and left for a separate targeted edit.
notes_for_decider: Safe to apply as a two-paragraph textual replacement in data/sections.json part5.html. Each replaced paragraph is a unique string match (verified occurrence count = 1 for each on 2026-04-22). No surrounding elements (details/summary/TLDR/before-after rule table/CSS-class paragraph/OTS concession paragraph) are touched. Word count grows from 275 to ~470 across both paragraphs — stays comfortably within normal section paragraph length. No code_analysis tags affected; section remains meta-commentary on a dated author edit. After integration, the c2 review's 8 holes should close as: H1, H2, H3, H4, H5, H6, H8 resolved; H7 (TLDR 'precisely') left for a separate edit. Recommend closing ISS-1288, ISS-1289, ISS-1290, ISS-1291, ISS-1292, ISS-1293, ISS-1295 on integration.
